On Wed, Jul 08, 2026 at 12:14:50PM +0800, Richard Chang wrote:
> On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 3:48\u202fAM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
> >
> > Can you please explain how freezing operation was delayed due to proactive
> > reclaim?
> >
> Hi Shakeel,
> 
> When system suspend is initiated, the Power Management (PM) freezer
> attempts to freeze all user-space processes by calling
> freeze_processes(), signaling user space processes to enter the
> refrigerator. When a user-space task writes to memory.reclaim to
> trigger proactive reclaim, it executes in kernel context and enters
> the reclaim loop. Currently the task does not check  signal_pending()
> within the inner loops, it cannot abort the reclaim pass early. It
> continues reclaiming memory, ignoring the freeze request. The PM
> freezer waits for all tasks to freeze. If the proactive reclaim task
> remains in the inner loops longer than the freeze timeout, the freezer
> times out, and suspend fails.

What's so special about proactive reclaim? Why the same situation can not happen
for global or memcg reclaim?

review_comments.author: Richard Chang
review_comments.summary: Explained the scenario where proactive reclaim delays freezing operation and proposed patch to abort proactive reclaim early when freezing is initiated.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 8, 2026 at 3:48 AM Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev> wrote:
>
> Can you please explain how freezing operation was delayed due to proactive
> reclaim?
>
Hi Shakeel,

When system suspend is initiated, the Power Management (PM) freezer
attempts to freeze all user-space processes by calling
freeze_processes(), signaling user space processes to enter the
refrigerator. When a user-space task writes to memory.reclaim to
trigger proactive reclaim, it executes in kernel context and enters
the reclaim loop. Currently the task does not check  signal_pending()
within the inner loops, it cannot abort the reclaim pass early. It
continues reclaiming memory, ignoring the freeze request. The PM
freezer waits for all tasks to freeze. If the proactive reclaim task
remains in the inner loops longer than the freeze timeout, the freezer
times out, and suspend fails.

review_comments.author: Ziyang Men
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Ziyang Men noted that the existing test in cgroup_iter_memcg.c only checks if stats are non-zero, but not their actual correctness, and suggested adding a comparison of BPF-based cgroup stats collection against memory.stat values. He also pointed out that the existing test does not verify the flush operation by comparing numbers after activity stops.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Eduard,

Thanks a lot for the review. Yes, as Shakeel mentioned, the performance
comparison for BPF-based cgroup stats collection was the original motivation.
But the patch also carries functional value: alongside that comparison, it
checks the correctness of the stats the kfuncs return.

Let me first answer the main question -- what these tests add over what we
already have -- and then lay out a plan.

First, the static test (memcg_stat_reader) vs the existing cgroup_iter_memcg.

The existing test calls the kfuncs, but for each value it only checks whether it
is greater than zero. For example, in prog_tests/cgroup_iter_memcg.c:

     memset(map, 1, len);                    /* dirty some anon */
     if (!ASSERT_OK(read_stats(link), "read stats"))
             goto cleanup;
     ASSERT_GT(memcg_query->nr_anon_mapped, 0, "final anon mapped val");

It never checks the value is actually correct -- i.e. compares it against the
value in cgroupfs -- only that it is non-zero.

Besides, it also walks a single cgroup:

     .cgroup.order = BPF_CGROUP_ITER_SELF_ONLY,

and reads only five fields.

The memcg_stat_reader in this patch adds three things:

   1. It compares the numbers. It reads the stats through the kfuncs and checks
      they match the values in memory.stat, instead of only checking they are
      non-zero (memcg_stat_reader.c, check_correctness()):

         /*
          * anon (NR_ANON_MAPPED) is rstat-flushed and, with the charger
          * stopped, deterministic: BPF and memory.stat must agree.
          */
         if ((b.anon > f.anon ? b.anon - f.anon
                              : f.anon - b.anon) > anon_tol)
                 anon_mism++;
         ...
         ASSERT_EQ(anon_mism, 0, "bpf vs file anon (rstat-flushed)");

      This is the main gap: b.anon comes from the kfunc, f.anon from parsing
      memory.stat, and the test requires them to agree.

   2. It covers a whole subtree, not a single cgroup:

         linfo.cgroup.order = BPF_CGROUP_ITER_DESCENDANTS_PRE;

   3. It reads a much broader field set (~40 fields, collect_full_stats()).
      Minor.

Second, the churn test adds something the static test cannot.

These counters are kept separately on each CPU for speed, and are only added
together when the code "flushes" them. The existing test does call the flush,
right before reading (progs/cgroup_iter_memcg.c):

     bpf_mem_cgroup_flush_stats(memcg);
     memcg_query.nr_anon_mapped = bpf_mem_cgroup_page_state(
             memcg, bpf_core_enum_value(enum node_stat_item, NR_ANON_MAPPED));

But if the cgroup is idle there is nothing to add up, so the flush does no real
work -- and since the result is only checked for non-zero, nothing verifies the
flush gathered anything. If bpf_mem_cgroup_flush_stats() were replaced by an
empty stub, this test would very likely still pass (the kernel also flushes on
its own, periodically and on thresholds).

With activity going on, especially spread across several CPUs, we can make the
flush do real work and then check it by comparing the numbers after the activity
stops. That flush check is missing from all of the current tests, and this is
where memcg_stat_churn / churn_percpu come in.

Finally, my plan:

   1. Keep the static (value-correctness) test, or fold it into cgroup_iter_memcg
      if that works better. I also noticed that several kfuncs are not called by
       any test today (no callers anywhere under tools/testing/selftests/bpf/,
       only __ksym declarations in vmlinux.h): bpf_get_root_mem_cgroup,
       bpf_mem_cgroup_usage, and bpf_mem_cgroup_memory_events. The static test
       would be a good place to cover them too.

   2. Keep only one of the two churn tests -- most likely the per-CPU one, since
      it is the stronger of the two -- and change its correctness check to
      actually verify the flush by comparing the numbers. The current check is
      weak; it only checks non-zero values and that every cgroup was visited:

         ASSERT_EQ(missing, 0, "all cgroups present in map");
         ASSERT_GT(total_anon, 0, "tree carries anon under churn");

   3. Move the code these tests share into a common header, to remove the
      duplication you pointed out. Or we can combine these two tests into a
      single one if works better.

   4. Keep the focus on functional testing in the selftests. The file-vs-BPF
      timing could stay as an extra bonus -- printed only as informational
      output in verbose mode, never as pass/fail -- but I'm happy to move that
      comparison to the bench framework if you think it fits better there.

Please let me know if you have any concerns with this plan. I am happy to take
any suggestions on how to improve the tests, or to change the focus of the
selftests

Thanks again,
Ziyang

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] eventpoll: compute timer slack lazily in ep_poll()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] eventpoll: compute timer slack lazily in ep_poll()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the eventpoll code by lazily computing the timer slack in ep_poll(). Instead of estimating the slack upfront, it is computed only when the thread is about to sleep. This reduces unnecessary overhead for busy pollers that often find events already pending and return without blocking. The estimation is deferred to the point where the thread actually sleeps, making it marginally more accurate. Measurements show that around 16% of ep_poll invocations have no consumer for the estimated slack, highlighting the inefficiency of the original approach.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] hrtimers: skip cpu_base->lock in hrtimer_get_next_event() when hres_active

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the hrtimer_get_next_event() function by skipping the cpu_base->lock acquisition when high resolution timers are active. The lock is not required because the hres_active flag is only written by the local CPU and all callers reach this point with interrupts disabled on the same CPU.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] mm/madvise: skip device-private PMDs in cold and pageout walks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a potential double-put bug in the madvise_cold_or_pageout_pte_range() function, which can occur when device-private PMDs are involved. The fix involves skipping device-private PMDs after taking the lock to prevent VM_BUG_ON() assertions. This change is necessary to avoid data corruption and ensure correct migration behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues caused by device-private PMDs in walk callbacks. Device-private PMDs can be created when an HMM-based GPU driver migrates anonymous THPs to device memory, leading to unexpected behavior and potential crashes. The patches modify various MM walk callbacks to skip or handle these device-private PMDs correctly, preventing VM_BUG_ONs, oops, and incorrect LRU isolation.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/11] mm: add PMD swap entry detection support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for detecting PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in the Linux kernel. Currently, when a huge page is swapped out, it's split into 512 PTE-level swap entries. To preserve huge page information across swap cycles, this patch introduces infrastructure to detect and handle PMD-level swap entries instead. The patch adds helper functions for architectures that support PMD softleaf entries and provides no-op fallbacks for those without such support. This is the first of 11 patches in a series aimed at improving memory management and preserving huge page information during swapping.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/11] mm: add PMD swap entry splitting support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for splitting a PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem. This is done by adding a new branch to the __split_huge_pmd_locked() function that handles PMD swap entries differently than migration splits. The patch preserves flags such as soft_dirty, uffd_wp, and exclusive when splitting the PMD swap entry into PTEs. The change also includes checks to prevent potential issues with uninitialized variables in the swap branch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in fork path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries during the fork process in Linux. It introduces a new function, `swap_dup_entries_direct`, which can duplicate a contiguous range of swap slots in one call. The patch also modifies the `copy_huge_non_present_pmd` function to 'copy' PMD swap entries instead of splitting them, preserving the Huge Page (THP). This change mirrors the behavior of `copy_nonpresent_pte` for PTEs and ensures that the PMD is stable across retries if necessary. The patch also includes modifications to handle GFP_ATOMIC table allocation failures and retries with GFP_KERNEL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/11] mm: zswap: add range lookup for large-folio swapin
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a range lookup feature to the zswap compression algorithm, allowing it to efficiently handle large folios (memory blocks) that span multiple pages. The patch introduces a new function `zswap_range_has_entry` that checks if any slot within a given range is present in zswap. This enables zswap to scan the covered range and return an error if any part of the large folio is still stored in zswap, preventing partially initialized data from being returned. The patch also updates the `zswap_load` function to use this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/11] mm: swap in PMD swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of swap entries during swapoff by introducing a new function, `swap_pmd_cache_lookup`, which classifies the swap cache behind a PMD swap entry as empty, backed by one PMD-sized folio, or requiring per-page handling. The patch also introduces a new function, `unuse_pmd`, which swaps in PMD-level swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff. If a PMD-order folio cannot be allocated, the PMD swap entry is split into PTE-level entries via `__split_huge_pmd`. This change aims to optimize swapoff performance by reducing the number of page-table lookups required.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in non-present PMD walkers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates various Linux kernel functions to handle PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in non-present PMD walkers, mirroring the behavior of PTE-level equivalents. The changes include adding a new helper function for accounting swap and swap_pss, updating several functions to handle swap entries alongside migration entries, and modifying functions that handle faults, madvise operations, and memory policy enforcement. The goal is to ensure correct handling of swapped-out THPs (Transparent Huge Pages) in various kernel paths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enhances the handling of PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED operations. It ensures that PMD-sized folios are properly prefetched and avoids unnecessary splitting of PMD swap entries. The patch introduces a new approach to handle PMD swap entries under pmd_trans_huge_lock(), which includes checking for split cache state, zswap entries, or existing PMD-sized folios. If any of these conditions are met, the patch splits the PMD swap entry and asks the walker to retry through the PTE path. Otherwise, it pins the swap device and reads the folio in at PMD order via swapin_sync(). This approach keeps the subsequent fault on the do_huge_pmd_swap_page() path and avoids order-0 readahead unnecessarily splitting the PMD swap entry.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in UFFDIO_MOVE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for moving PMD-level swap entries in the UFFDIO_MOVE operation. It introduces a new function, move_swap_pmd(), which moves the swap entry whole-PMD and re-anchors the PMD-sized swap-cache folio's anon rmap to the destination VMA. The patch also updates the dispatcher in move_pages_huge_pmd() to wait for migration on a PMD migration entry and route PMD swap entries through move_swap_pmd(). This ensures that UFFDIO_MOVE operations can handle PMD swap entries correctly, even when they are swapped out.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/11] mm: handle PMD swap entry faults on swap-in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new handler for PMD swap entry faults during the swap-in process. The handler, do_huge_pmd_swap_page(), resolves the entire 2 MB mapping in one shot by looking up the folio in the swap cache and allocating a PMD-order folio if necessary. It then re-validates the folio and restores soft_dirty and uffd_wp from the swap PMD. If the resulting PMD is read-only but the fault was a write, it updates vmf->orig_pmd and calls wp_huge_pmd() to COW immediately. The patch also handles cases where PMD-order resources are unavailable by splitting the PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries via __split_huge_pmd().

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the patch does not handle the case where a PMD swap entry is split into PTEs, and requested that the handling of this case be added.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the patch does a synchronous swap-in without readahead, which makes sense due to the PMD size, but suggested mentioning this in the commit message for clarity and proposed using the return value of swapin_sync directly instead of handling its possible values separately.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that repeated faults may occur when trying to lock a PMD-sized folio on a slower device, potentially leading to performance issues, and asked if this problem will worsen for PMDs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Usama

Amazing work.

---

Hmm, so here we always do a sync swapin which means there is no
readahead, kind of make sense since PMD is huge, readahead might be
not helpful and a order 0 readahead breaks following PMD. But maybe
worth mention it in the commit message at least so it's easiler to
follow. (partly because the name swapin_sync is a bit confusing at the
moment, we could change that name as we improve swap readahead later).

And just an idea that will it be suffient if we just use the return
value of swapin_sync directly? If a smaller folio covers the PMD start
entry, the smaller folio is returned and you catched that just fine.
If a smaller folio covers a follow-up sub-entry or a sub-entry is
freed, -EBUSY is returned here and you just do the split. And it
doesn't do the allocation for the first lookup check, so there should
be no thrashing issue.

---

With PMD sized folio on a slower device, this try lock could fail much
more frequently, leading to repeated faults. That's not a new issue
and I remember Barry talked about it at the LSFMM this year, just I'm
not sure if that will get much worse for PMD?

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2

Author acknowledged a concern about readahead not being useful for PMDs and agreed to add it to the commit message.

The author addressed a concern about zswap handling in PMD swap entry faults, explaining that zswap stores ranges as order-0 objects which can lead to non-updated large folios in the cache and subsequent SIGBUS errors.

The author acknowledged a potential issue with PMD-sized IO keeping the folio locked longer on slow swap devices, but agreed that the current behavior matches do_swap_page() and is not a tight retry loop. They suggested any improvement belongs to broader swap readahead/async swapin work.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kairui,

Thanks! and Thanks for the review comments!

---

Yes, I\u2019ll add this to the commit message. Thanks!

Yes readahead won't be useful for PMD as you said.

---

The main issue over here is zswap.
zswap still stores the range as order-0 objects. If we call PMD-order
swapin_sync() when the cache is empty but any slot is in zswap, zswap_load()
fails the large-folio load and leaves a non-uptodate PMD-sized folio in
swapcache. Splitting the PMD after that is not enough unless we also delete that
failed large folio, because the subsequent PTE fault can find the non-uptodate
large folio and SIGBUS instead of loading the individual zswap entries.

---

Agreed that PMD-sized IO can keep the folio locked longer, especially on
slow swap devices. (Which hopefully are dying away :))
The current behavior matches do_swap_page(): on the normal retryable fault
path it drops the fault lock and waits for the folio unlock before returning
VM_FAULT_RETRY, so it should not be a tight retry loop.
The wait can be longer for 2MB IO, but the retry usually comes back to
either an uptodate folio or a PMD that another fault already  installed.
I think this is the right baseline behavior, and any improvement here
belongs with the broader swap readahead / async swapin work.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/11] mm: install PMD swap entries on swap-out
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the swap-out process for huge pages by installing PMD-level swap entries instead of splitting them into individual PTEs, reducing overhead and improving performance. It mirrors the existing PTE swap-out path at PMD granularity, handling zswap and adjusting MM_ANONPAGES / MM_SWAPENTS accordingly. The patch introduces a new function set_pmd_swap_entry() to install the swap PMD, which preserves original soft-dirty, uffd-wp, and exclusive bits. This change aims to improve the efficiency of huge page swapping and reduce the load on khugepaged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/11] selftests/mm: add PMD swap entry tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test suite to exercise PMD swap entry paths in the Linux kernel. The tests cover various scenarios, including swapping out and in of THPs, fork operations, write faults, munmap, mprotect, UFFDIO_MOVE, mremap, pagemap reading, mincore, MADV_FREE, MADV_WILLNEED, and swapoff. The test suite also accounts for the presence or absence of zswap, which can affect how PMD swap entries are handled. The tests verify that data correctness is maintained across these operations and that the kernel behaves as expected in different configurations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new feature to Linux kernel called PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs (Transparent Huge Pages). When a PMD-mapped anonymous THP is swapped out, the current implementation splits it into PTE-level swap entries. This patch series changes this behavior so that a single PMD-level swap entry is created instead, allowing the huge mapping to survive the swap round-trip and be restored directly on swap-in without needing khugepaged to collapse the range later. The PMD swap entry is a compact page-table encoding for HPAGE_PMD_NR consecutive swap slots, and its handling is designed to work seamlessly with existing kernel features like zswap and khugepaged.
